title: World Literature
intro: World Literature, as tested in quizbowl, gathers the literary traditions of Latin America, Africa, the Middle East, South and East Asia, the Caribbean, and Oceania, along with a comparative layer of continental European and classical Greco-Roman material. The single most heavily asked cluster is the Latin American Boom and its magical-realist wing: Jorge Luis Borges, Pablo Neruda, Gabriel Garcia Marquez, and Julio Cortazar recur constantly, alongside their national literatures of Argentina, Chile, Mexico, and Colombia. A second major cluster covers Africa, led by Nigerian writers Wole Soyinka and Chinua Achebe and South African writers Nadine Gordimer, J.M. Coetzee, and Athol Fugard, with postcolonialism, apartheid, and Negritude as recurring thematic threads.

East and South Asia form comparably deep clusters. Japanese literature spans classical court writing (The Pillow Book, The Tale of Genji), the modern Nobel laureates Kawabata, Oe, and Mishima, and the contemporary Murakami, while Chinese literature ranges from Tang dynasty poets Li Bai and Du Fu through the Four Great Classical Novels to twentieth-century figures like Lu Xun and Mo Yan. South Asian questions cluster around Rabindranath Tagore and classical Sanskrit drama on one end and Salman Rushdie, Arundhati Roy, and Vikram Seth on the other. Middle Eastern and Persian literature is anchored by Naguib Mahfouz, Rumi, and Orhan Pamuk, with Arabic and Persian poetic forms like the ghazal recurring as identifiers.

Smaller but consistent clusters cover Caribbean literature (Derek Walcott, V.S. Naipaul, Aime Cesaire) and the Commonwealth literatures of Australia, Canada, and New Zealand (Patrick White, Katherine Mansfield, Margaret Atwood, Alice Munro, Peter Carey). A comparative layer of continental European writers -- Kafka, Tolstoy, Goethe, Camus -- and classical Greek and Roman authors also recurs, usually alongside the non-Western canon rather than as the unit's primary focus. Beyond named authors and works, a substantial share of questions in this unit are common-link tossups built around a shared object, animal, or theme -- tigers, masks, mirrors, suicide, rivers -- that spans several national literatures, or around movement terms like magical realism, the Latin American Boom, and Negritude that describe groups of writers rather than single answerlines.

sections.name: Latin American Literature
sections.blurb: The Latin American Boom and its magical-realist inheritors dominate this unit, anchored by Borges, Neruda, and García Márquez.

entries.note: Argentine master of labyrinthine, philosophical short fiction; wrote "The Garden of Forking Paths" and Ficciones.
Chilean Nobel laureate poet of Twenty Love Poems and Canto General; wrote surrealist verse and political odes.
Portuguese-speaking South American nation; home to Machado de Assis, Clarice Lispector, and Jorge Amado.
Home to Borges, Cortazar, and the gaucho epic Martin Fierro; Buenos Aires-centered literary tradition.
Argentine Boom author of Hopscotch and the story collections behind "Blow-Up"; masterful short fiction.
Home to Octavio Paz, Juan Rulfo's Pedro Paramo, and Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz.
Mexican Nobel poet of The Labyrinth of Solitude and "Sunstone"; associated with Surrealism and Modernismo.
Chilean magical realist novelist of The House of the Spirits and Eva Luna; niece of Salvador Allende.
Colombian Nobel laureate of One Hundred Years of Solitude and Love in the Time of Cholera; father of magical realism.
Home to Jose Marti's Modernismo and Nicolas Guillen's Afro-Cuban poetry.
Peruvian Nobel laureate of the Latin American Boom; wrote Aunt Julia and the Scriptwriter and The Time of the Hero.
Chilean Nobel laureate poet, first Latin American to win; wrote Sonnets of Death; associated with Modernismo.
Nicaraguan founder of the Modernismo movement; wrote the poetry collection Azul....
Mexican Boom novelist of The Death of Artemio Cruz and Terra Nostra; wrote the historical novel The Old Gringo.
Isabel Allende's magical realist multigenerational Chilean family saga, later a film.
Chilean novelist of the posthumously published 2666 and The Savage Detectives; associated with Infrarealism.
Home to Neruda, Mistral, Bolano, and Isabel Allende.
Language of Brazilian literature, including Machado de Assis and Clarice Lispector.

entries.topic: Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz
entries.answerline: Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz
entries.note: 17th-century Mexican nun-poet of colonial New Spain; wrote "First Dream" and defended women's education.
Gabriel Garcia Marquez's landmark magical realist saga of the Buendia family in Macondo.
Full name of the Caribbean island nation home to Jose Marti and Nicolas Guillen.
Cuban novelist who coined "lo real maravilloso"; wrote The Kingdom of This World and Explosion in a Cathedral.
Mario Vargas Llosa's semi-autobiographical novel of a young writer and a radio-serial scriptwriter, Pedro Camacho.
Brazilian novelist of existentialist stream-of-consciousness prose; wrote The Hour of the Star and The Passion According to G.H.
Uruguayan short-story writer known for macabre tales like "The Feather Pillow," often compared to Poe.
Brazilian realist novelist of Dom Casmurro and The Posthumous Memoirs of Bras Cubas.
Cuban independence hero and Modernismo poet; wrote the essay "Our America" and the poem behind "Guantanamera."

entries.topic: Love in the Time of Cholera
entries.answerline: Love in the Time of Cholera
entries.note: Garcia Marquez's novel of a decades-long unrequited love in a Colombian river town.
Full name of the nation home to Machado de Assis and Clarice Lispector.
Peruvian modernist poet known for avant-garde, anguished verse.
Garcia Marquez's novella reconstructing a small-town honor killing everyone knew was coming.
Full name of the nation home to Neruda, Mistral, and Bolano.
Roberto Bolano's sprawling posthumous novel centered on murders in a fictional Mexican border city.
Julio Cortazar's experimental novel readable in multiple chapter orders; Boom-era masterpiece.
Clarice Lispector's final novel about a poor, unremarkable typist named Macabea in Rio.
Octavio Paz's long circular poem structured on the Aztec calendar, meditating on time and love.
Machado de Assis's Brazilian novel narrated by a jealous husband, Bentinho, suspicious of his wife Capitu.
Brazilian novelist of Gabriela, Clove and Cinnamon and Dona Flor and Her Two Husbands.
Fictional Colombian town in Garcia Marquez's One Hundred Years of Solitude, founded by Jose Arcadio Buendia.
Alejo Carpentier's novel of the Haitian Revolution, coining the concept of "lo real maravilloso."

entries.topic: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair
entries.answerline: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair
entries.note: Pablo Neruda's early, highly popular collection of romantic and erotic Chilean poetry.
Home to Horacio Quiroga's macabre short fiction.
Borges story of a labyrinthine novel-within-a-story about branching, parallel time.
Borges story about a fictional encyclopedia entry that begins to overwrite reality.
Miguel Angel Asturias's novel about a Latin American dictator, a founding work of the "dictator novel."
Isabel Allende's magical realist novel about a Chilean storyteller narrating her own picaresque life.
Argentine novelist of Kiss of the Spider Woman, set among cellmates in a Latin American prison.
Guatemalan Nobel laureate of El Senor Presidente and Men of Maize.
Juan Rulfo's Mexican novel in which a man discovers his father's town is populated by ghosts.
Home to Mario Vargas Llosa and Cesar Vallejo.
Language of the Latin American Boom and much of Latin America's literary tradition.
Borges story about a point in space containing all other points, glimpsed in a Buenos Aires basement.
Argentine writer and close friend/collaborator of Borges; wrote The Invention of Morel.
Full name of the Mexican Boom novelist who wrote The Death of Artemio Cruz.
Ariel Dorfman's play about a Chilean woman who believes her guest tortured her under the dictatorship.
Argentine cowboy figures central to the epic poem Martin Fierro.
Mexican novelist of Pedro Paramo, a foundational text for magical realism.
Laura Esquivel's Mexican novel where a woman's cooking magically transmits her emotions.
Chilean creator of "antipoetry," deflating poetic solemnity with colloquial irony.
Carlos Fuentes's novel narrated by a dying tycoon reviewing his corruption after the Mexican Revolution.
Carlos Fuentes's novel imagining Ambrose Bierce's disappearance into revolutionary Mexico.
Borges story imagining the universe as an infinite library containing every possible book.
Brazilian realist of Dom Casmurro and The Posthumous Memoirs of Bras Cubas.
Adolfo Bioy Casares's novella of a fugitive on an island haunted by holographic recordings.
Concluding poem of Neruda's Twenty Love Poems, lamenting a lost love from a harbor.
Dying Mexican tycoon who reviews his corrupted revolutionary past in Fuentes's novel.
Elusive German novelist sought by critics in Roberto Bolano's 2666.
Dead narrator of Machado de Assis's Posthumous Memoirs, dedicating the book to the worm that first gnawed him.
Argentine capital, literary home of Borges, Cortazar, and the Sur circle.
Pablo Neruda's collection praising ordinary things like socks, salt, and an onion.
Manuel Puig's novel of cellmates Molina and Valentin, told largely through recounted films.
Jose Hernandez's Argentine epic poem of a persecuted gaucho outlaw.
Home of Modernismo founder Ruben Dario and priest-poet Ernesto Cardenal.
Cuban national poet of Afro-Cuban rhythm-infused verse; leader of Negrismo.
Full name of the Mexican Nobel poet of The Labyrinth of Solitude.
Full name of the Chilean author of 2666 and The Savage Detectives.
Refers to Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz, 17th-century Mexican nun-poet of "First Dream."
Mario Vargas Llosa's novel of the Trujillo dictatorship and assassination in the Dominican Republic.
Octavio Paz's essay collection analyzing Mexican identity, masks, and solitude.
Roberto Bolano's novel tracking "visceral realist" poets Arturo Belano and Ulises Lima across decades.
Vargas Llosa's debut novel of cadets and a cover-up at Lima's Leoncio Prado military academy.
Argentine feminist poet who drowned herself in the sea at Mar del Plata.
Refers to Isabel Allende, Chilean author of The House of the Spirits.
Chilean playwright of Death and the Maiden, confronting torture after dictatorship.
Guatemalan master of micro-fiction, including the one-sentence story "The Dinosaur."
Ruben Dario's 1888 poetry-and-prose collection that launched Modernismo.
Home of Gabriel Garcia Marquez and the fictional Macondo.
Buendia son who faces the firing squad in One Hundred Years of Solitude's famous opening; fought 32 wars.

entries.topic: Dona Flor and Her Two Husbands
entries.answerline: Dona Flor and Her Two Husbands
entries.note: Jorge Amado's Brazilian novel of a widow whose roguish first husband returns as a ghost.
Nicaraguan priest-poet of liberation theology; wrote Zero Hour and founded Solentiname.
Refers to Julio Cortazar, Argentine author of Hopscotch.
Full name of the Argentine Boom author of Hopscotch and "Blow-Up."
Mexican author of Like Water for Chocolate, where Tita's cooking transmits her emotions.
Refers to Argentine sisters Silvina Ocampo, fantastical story writer, and Victoria, founder of Sur.
Manic Bolivian radio-serial scriptwriter of Vargas Llosa's Aunt Julia and the Scriptwriter.
Gabriela Mistral's prize-winning sonnets mourning her lover Romelio Ureta's suicide.

entries.topic: The Posthumous Memoirs of Bras Cubas
entries.answerline: The Posthumous Memoirs of Bras Cubas
entries.note: Machado de Assis's novel narrated by a dead man, digressive and ironically self-aware.
Cortazar story of a photographer who discovers hidden menace in an enlarged photo; filmed by Antonioni.
Borges detective story of Lonnrot lured by a kabbalistic pattern to the villa Triste-le-Roy.
Sor Juana's long philosophical poem of the soul's nighttime flight toward knowledge.
Cuban song whose lyrics adapt Jose Marti's Versos Sencillos.
Cortazar story of siblings gradually displaced from their home by an unseen presence.
Jose Marti's essay urging Latin America to forge its own identity apart from the United States.
Horacio Quiroga horror story of a bride drained nightly by a parasite hidden in her pillow.
Manuel Puig's novel about a boy's escapist obsession with Hollywood films in provincial Argentina.
Pablo Neruda's epic poem cycle surveying the history and nature of Latin America.
Prolific Argentine experimental novelist known for short, surreal improvisational fiction.
Alejo Carpentier's novel about the French Revolution's reverberations in the Caribbean.
Narrator of Clarice Lispector's The Passion According to G.H., unraveled after killing a cockroach.
Jorge Amado's Brazilian novel of a widowed bar owner's romance with a mixed-race cook, Gabriela.
American translator renowned for English versions of Garcia Marquez's and Cortazar's fiction.
Full name of the Chilean magical realist novelist of The House of the Spirits.
Full name of the Cuban independence hero and Modernismo poet Jose Marti.
Region encompassing the Boom, magical realism, and Modernismo movements.
Uruguayan writer known for the novel La Tregua and politically engaged poetry.
Gypsy sage who brings inventions and prophecy to Macondo in One Hundred Years of Solitude.
Miguel Angel Asturias's novel blending Mayan myth with the exploitation of Guatemalan peasants.
Mexican capital and literary center for Paz, Fuentes, and Rulfo.
Refers to Gabriela Mistral, Chilean Nobel laureate poet of Sonnets of Death.
Full name of the nation home to Horacio Quiroga's macabre short fiction.
K'iche' Maya creation epic featuring the Hero Twins Hunahpu and Xbalanque.
Machado de Assis novel named for the mad philosopher of "Humanitas"; follows heir Rubiao.
Dominican dictator depicted in Vargas Llosa's Feast of the Goat and Diaz's Oscar Wao.
Full name of the nation home to Gabriel Garcia Marquez.
Railway worker whose suicide inspired Gabriela Mistral's Sonnets of Death.
Victoria Ocampo's influential Buenos Aires literary magazine, an early publisher of Borges.
Carlos Fuentes's vast novel refracting Spanish history through Philip II's Escorial.
Paulo Coelho's Brazilian fable of the shepherd Santiago pursuing his Personal Legend to Egypt.

entries.topic: The Devil to Pay in the Backlands
entries.answerline: The Devil to Pay in the Backlands
entries.note: Guimaraes Rosa's Brazilian sertao epic narrated by the bandit Riobaldo, haunted by a possible pact.
Clarice Lispector's mystical monologue of a woman transformed after crushing a cockroach.
Cabrera Infante's pun-drenched novel of pre-revolutionary Havana nightlife.
Chilean founder of Creacionismo; wrote the avant-garde long poem Altazor.

sections.name: African Literature
sections.blurb: Nigerian and South African writers lead a broad postcolonial African tradition spanning oral epic to apartheid-era fiction.

entries.note: Nigerian playwright and first African Nobel laureate in Literature; wrote Death and the King's Horseman and The Lion and the Jewel.
Nigerian novelist of Things Fall Apart, No Longer at Ease, and Arrow of God; chronicled Igbo society under colonialism.
Kenyan novelist of Petals of Blood and Wizard of the Crow; later wrote in Gikuyu, e.g. I Will Marry When I Want.
South African Nobel laureate chronicling apartheid; wrote Burger's Daughter and July's People.
Home to Achebe, Soyinka, and Adichie; Igbo and Yoruba literary traditions and postcolonial fiction.
South African playwright of "Master Harold"...and the Boys and Blood Knot; worked with the Serpent Players.
Nigerian novelist of Purple Hibiscus, Half of a Yellow Sun, and Americanah; feminist and postcolonial themes.
Home to Gordimer, Coetzee, and Fugard; apartheid-era literature is a defining theme.
Wole Soyinka's tragedy of a Yoruba chief horseman prevented from ritual suicide by colonial officials.
Wole Soyinka's comedy about a village belle courted by a modernizing schoolteacher and the traditional Bale.
Chinua Achebe's novel of Okonkwo, an Igbo leader undone by colonialism and his own rigidity.
Alan Paton's novel of a Zulu priest searching for his son amid apartheid-era South Africa.
Dutch-derived language of white South African literature, including some apartheid-era works.
Continent home to Nigerian, South African, Kenyan, and Egyptian literary traditions.
West African nation with a literary tradition including Ama Ata Aidoo.
Nigerian magical realist novelist of The Famished Road, narrated by the spirit-child Azaro.
J.M. Coetzee's Booker-winning novel of a disgraced professor and his daughter in post-apartheid South Africa.
Full name of the nation home to Achebe, Soyinka, and Adichie.
West African oral historian-poet-musicians who preserve epics like Sundiata.
Full name of the South African Nobel laureate who wrote Disgrace and Waiting for the Barbarians.
West African nation home to Negritude poet Leopold Senghor and filmmaker-novelist Ousmane Sembene.
British-Zimbabwean Nobel laureate known for The Golden Notebook; wrote about colonial Africa and feminism.
South African Nobel laureate of Disgrace, Waiting for the Barbarians, and Elizabeth Costello.
Nadine Gordimer's novel imagining a white South African family fleeing a Black revolutionary uprising.
Home to Ngugi wa Thiong'o; Gikuyu and English-language literary traditions.
Full name of the nation home to Ngugi wa Thiong'o.
Home to novelists like NoViolet Bulawayo and Doris Lessing's colonial-era fiction.
Tanzanian-British Nobel laureate known for novels of migration and colonialism, including Paradise.
South African author of Cry, the Beloved Country, about a Zulu priest and apartheid-era injustice.
Athol Fugard's play about two mixed-race South African half-brothers confronting apartheid's racial lines.
Tsitsi Dangarembga's Zimbabwean novel about a girl's education and struggle against colonial and patriarchal norms.
Congolese novelist writing in French, known for satirical postcolonial fiction.
Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie's novel of a Nigerian woman's experience of race in America and her return home.
Nigerian novelist known for depicting women's struggles, including in The Joys of Motherhood.
Nadine Gordimer's novel about the daughter of a white anti-apartheid activist in South Africa.
Nigerian modernist poet killed in the Nigerian Civil War; wrote Heavensgate.
Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie's novel set during the Nigerian Civil War and the Biafran secession.
Chinua Achebe's novel of Obi Okonkwo, an educated Nigerian undone by bribery in colonial Lagos.
Proud Igbo wrestler-farmer protagonist of Things Fall Apart, who hangs himself as colonialism advances.
Founder of the Mali Empire, hero of the griot-transmitted Epic of Sundiata.
Andre Brink's novel of a white South African teacher investigating a Black janitor's death in custody.
North African nation, setting of Camus's The Stranger and home to Assia Djebar.
Nigerian author of The Palm-Wine Drinkard, drawing on Yoruba folktales in idiosyncratic English.
Chinua Achebe's novel of the priest Ezeulu caught between Igbo religion and colonial authority.
J.M. Coetzee's fictional Australian novelist, delivering lectures in her eponymous novel.
East African empire whose Solomonic legend is told in the Kebra Nagast.
Full name of the nation home to Gordimer, Coetzee, and Fugard.
Full name of the nation home to Tsitsi Dangarembga and NoViolet Bulawayo.
Full name of the Nigerian Nobel playwright of Death and the King's Horseman.
Griot-transmitted Mande epic of the lion prince who founded the Mali Empire.
Senegalese Negritude poet and first president of Senegal.
Senegalese poet-president who co-founded Negritude with Aime Cesaire.
Senegalese novelist of So Long a Letter, a widow's letter on polygamy and women's lives.
Gardener protagonist of Coetzee's Life & Times of Michael K, wandering war-torn South Africa.
Refers to Ngugi wa Thiong'o, Kenyan author of Petals of Blood and Wizard of the Crow.
Senegalese novelist-filmmaker of God's Bits of Wood, on the Dakar-Niger railway strike.
Ngugi wa Thiong'o's novel of four suspects and a fire, indicting neocolonial Kenya.
Full name of the West African nation home to Ama Ata Aidoo.
Full name of the nation of Senghor, Mariama Ba, and Ousmane Sembene.
East African lingua franca with its own epic verse tradition; Ngugi contrasted it with colonial English.
Ben Okri's Booker-winning novel narrated by Azaro, a spirit-child in a Nigerian ghetto.
NoViolet Bulawayo's novel of Darling's childhood in Zimbabwe and emigration to America.
Ngugi wa Thiong'o's satirical epic of the imaginary dictatorship of Aburiria.
Chinua Achebe's lecture-essay condemning the racism of Conrad's Heart of Darkness.
Ghanaian writer of Our Sister Killjoy and the play The Dilemma of a Ghost.
Afrikaner novelist of A Dry White Season, opposing apartheid in both Afrikaans and English.
Afrikaans poet imprisoned for anti-apartheid activity; wrote True Confessions of an Albino Terrorist.
Charlatan beach prophet of Soyinka's comedy The Trials of Brother Jero.
Publisher whose African Writers Series, launched with Things Fall Apart, canonized African fiction.

entries.topic: I Will Marry When I Want
entries.answerline: I Will Marry When I Want
entries.note: Ngugi's Gikuyu-language protest play whose staging led to his detention without trial.
Nigerian Ogoni activist-author of Sozaboy, executed by the Abacha regime in 1995.
Athol Fugard's play of the white teen Hally's cruelty toward servants Sam and Willie.
Mozambican novelist of Sleepwalking Land, blending Portuguese with magical folk elements.
1967-70 Biafran conflict backdropping Half of a Yellow Sun; killed poet Christopher Okigbo.
Zimbabwean novelist of We Need New Names and the Gukurahundi satire Glory.
Somali novelist of Maps and the Variations on the Theme of an African Dictatorship trilogy.
Coetzee's novel of a frontier Magistrate complicit in an unnamed Empire's cruelty.
Ngugi's novel of the boy Njoroge's shattered hopes during Kenya's Mau Mau uprising.
Jose Eduardo Agualusa's novel of a woman who bricks herself into a Luanda apartment for decades.
Ngugi's novel of Mugo's hidden betrayal, set on the eve of Kenyan independence.

entries.topic: Ah, But Your Land is Beautiful
entries.answerline: Ah, But Your Land is Beautiful
entries.note: Bessie Head's novel-in-stories about apartheid-era Botswana and South Africa.
South African-Botswanan novelist known for A Question of Power and When Rain Clouds Gather.
Nigerian novelist of People of the City and Jagua Nana, depicting urban Nigerian life.
The King's Horseman of Soyinka's play, whose ritual duty to die is interrupted by colonial officials.
Kenyan language Ngugi wa Thiong'o adopted for his later fiction and plays.
Ethiopian sacred text recounting the Solomonic dynasty's origin from Solomon and Sheba.
Nigerian-American short-story writer known for What It Means When a Man Falls from the Sky.
West African empire founded by Sundiata Keita, subject of the griot-preserved epic.
Southeast African nation home to novelist Mia Couto.
South African anti-apartheid leader and president; subject of Long Walk to Freedom.
Grandson of Achebe's Okonkwo, protagonist of No Longer at Ease navigating corruption in colonial Nigeria.
Drink central to Tutuola's Palm-Wine Drinkard, whose hero seeks his dead tapster in the spirit world.
Adichie's debut novel of Kambili's coming of age under a devout, abusive Nigerian father.
Multiracial Port Elizabeth theater troupe Athol Fugard worked with under apartheid.
Fugard-Kani-Ntshona play of a man adopting a dead man's passbook to survive apartheid.
Home of Tayeb Salih, author of Season of Migration to the North.
Amos Tutuola's Yoruba-folklore novel of a drinker pursuing his dead palm-wine tapster.
Johannesburg priest who guides Stephen Kumalo in Cry, the Beloved Country.
East African nation of Okot p'Bitek, author of Song of Lawino.
Nigerian people and language whose mythology shapes Soyinka's and Tutuola's work.
South African novelist-playwright of Ways of Dying, featuring the professional mourner Toloki.

sections.name: Middle Eastern & Persian Literature
sections.blurb: Arabic, Persian, and Turkish traditions cluster around Mahfouz's Cairo, Sufi poets like Rumi, and Pamuk's Istanbul.

entries.note: Egyptian Nobel laureate known for the Cairo Trilogy; also wrote Children of Gebelawi and The Thief and the Dogs.
Turkish Nobel laureate of My Name is Red, Snow, and The Museum of Innocence; Istanbul-centered postmodernism.
Language of Mahfouz, One Thousand and One Nights, and classical poetic forms.
Language of Rumi, Hafez, Omar Khayyam, and the Shahnameh epic.
Home to Naguib Mahfouz and the Cairo-set Cairo Trilogy; Arabic literary center.
13th-century Persian Sufi mystic poet; wrote the Masnavi, the Spiritual Couplets.
Orhan Pamuk's novel set in the Turkish city of Kars, exploring political Islam and secularism.
Palestinian national poet known for resistance poetry like "Identity Card."
Egyptian capital and setting of Naguib Mahfouz's Cairo Trilogy and Palace Walk.
Orhan Pamuk's murder mystery set among Ottoman miniaturist painters in 16th-century Istanbul.
Ferdowsi's Persian national epic, the "Book of Kings," chronicling Iran's mythical and historical rulers.
Marjane Satrapi's autobiographical graphic novel about growing up during the Iranian Revolution.
Home to Rumi, Ferdowsi, Hafez, and modern writers like Marjane Satrapi.
Home to Kahlil Gibran, author of The Prophet.
Khaled Hosseini's novel of a boy's guilt over betraying his friend Hassan in Afghanistan.
Persian and Urdu lyric poetic form of rhyming couplets, perfected by Hafez and Ghalib.
Revived modern literary language of Israeli writers like Amos Oz and Yehuda Amichai.
Persian polymath poet whose quatrains were rendered famous in English as The Rubaiyat.

entries.topic: Season of Migration to the North
entries.answerline: Season of Migration to the North
entries.note: Tayeb Salih's Sudanese novel of a man's disorienting return home after studying in England.
Kahlil Gibran's poetic prose collection of philosophical essays on love, work, and life.
Home to Orhan Pamuk; Istanbul-centered modern literary tradition.
Home to modern Hebrew-language writers like Amos Oz and Yehuda Amichai.
Edward FitzGerald's famous English translation of Omar Khayyam's Persian quatrains.
Turkish city central to Orhan Pamuk's novels, including My Name is Red and The Museum of Innocence.
Full name of the 13th-century Persian Sufi poet who wrote the Masnavi.
Storyteller of One Thousand and One Nights who delays her execution by telling tales nightly.
City central to Hebrew and Israeli literature, including works by Amos Oz.
Orhan Pamuk's novel of Kemal's obsessive love for Fusun, memorialized in a real Istanbul museum.
Mahmoud Darwish's defiant resistance poem beginning "Write down: I am an Arab."
Israeli novelist of A Tale of Love and Darkness; leading modern Hebrew writer and peace advocate.
Palestinian-American critic whose Orientalism founded postcolonial critique of Western views of the East.
Modern name for Persian, language of Rumi, Hafez, and the Shahnameh.
Afghan-American novelist of The Kite Runner and A Thousand Splendid Suns.
Nizami's Persian tragic romance of a poet driven mad by forbidden love.
Arabic frame-tale collection in which Scheherazade tells stories to postpone her execution.
National identity central to the poetry of Mahmoud Darwish.
Leading modern Hebrew poet of everyday Jerusalem life and war's aftermath.
Naguib Mahfouz's banned allegorical novel retelling Abrahamic religious history in a Cairo alley.
Persian-Urdu lyric form of rhyming couplets on love and loss, mastered by Hafez and Ghalib.
14th-century Persian lyric poet of Shiraz whose Divan perfected the ghazal.
Persian rhyming-couplet form; capitalized, Rumi's vast Sufi poem of that name.
English name for Rumi's Masnavi, the vast Persian Sufi didactic poem.
Islamic mystical tradition animating the poetry of Rumi, Hafez, and Attar.
Omar Khayyam's Persian quatrains on wine and mortality, famed via FitzGerald's translation.
Alternate title of One Thousand and One Nights, Scheherazade's frame-tale collection.
Persian poet who spent decades composing the Shahnameh, Iran's national epic.
Iranian-French graphic memoirist of Persepolis, on growing up through the Iranian Revolution.
First novel of Mahfouz's Cairo Trilogy, on the tyrannical patriarch al-Sayyid Ahmad's family.
Home of the modernist Arabic poet Adonis.
Egyptian pioneer of Arabic drama; wrote The People of the Cave.
Pen name of Ali Ahmad Said, Syrian modernist poet who transformed Arabic verse.
Alternate spelling of Adonis, the Syrian modernist Arabic poet.
Setting of Khaled Hosseini's The Kite Runner and A Thousand Splendid Suns.
Full name of the nation home to Naguib Mahfouz.
Iranian-American memoirist of Reading Lolita in Tehran, about a secret women's literature class.
Lebanese capital, literary center of the Arabic-language Levant.
Sa'di's Persian prose-and-verse work of moral anecdotes, the "Rose Garden."
Afghan capital, central setting of Khaled Hosseini's The Kite Runner.
Lebanese-American poet of The Prophet, blending mysticism and philosophical prose poetry.
Alternate spelling of Kahlil Gibran, Lebanese-American poet of The Prophet.
Revived literary language of Israeli writers like Amos Oz and Yehuda Amichai.
Persian poet who wrote the tragic romance Layla and Majnun.
Turkish imperial backdrop for Pamuk's My Name is Red and Ottoman miniaturist culture.
Persian poet of Shiraz who wrote the Gulistan and Bustan, blending moral tales with verse.
Mythical Persian bird sought by the pilgrims of Attar's Conference of the Birds.
Full name of the nation home to Amos Oz and Yehuda Amichai.
Moroccan Francophone novelist of The Sand Child and its Goncourt-winning sequel The Sacred Night.
Iranian capital of Nafisi's Reading Lolita in Tehran and Persepolis's setting.
Sadegh Hedayat's surreal, opium-hazed Iranian novel of a pen-case painter's obsessions.
Attar's Sufi allegory of birds crossing seven valleys to find the Simurgh -- themselves.
Mahfouz's noir novel of Said Mahran's doomed revenge after release from prison.
Language of Orhan Pamuk and modern Turkey's literature.

sections.name: South & Southeast Asian Literature
sections.blurb: Indian writers in English and Bengali, alongside classical Sanskrit drama and Filipino and Indonesian fiction, run from Kalidasa to Rushdie.

entries.note: Bengali polymath, first non-European Nobel laureate in Literature; wrote Gitanjali and The Home and the World.
Arundhati Roy's Booker Prize novel of forbidden love and twins in Kerala, India.
Indian-British magical realist of Midnight's Children and The Satanic Verses.
Rabindranath Tagore's "Song Offerings" poetry collection that won him the 1913 Nobel Prize.
Classical Sanskrit poet-dramatist; wrote The Recognition of Shakuntala and other court dramas.
Language of Tagore's Gitanjali and The Home and the World; major literature of eastern India and Bangladesh.
Indian novelist of A Suitable Boy and the verse novel The Golden Gate; New Formalist poet.
Classical Indian literary language of Kalidasa's dramas and the epics Ramayana and Mahabharata.
Indian-American author of Interpreter of Maladies and "Sexy"; explores Bengali immigrant experience.
Salman Rushdie's Booker of Bookers-winning novel of Saleem Sinai, born at India's independence.
Home to Tagore, Rushdie, Roy, and Seth; Hindi, Bengali, Tamil, and English-language traditions.
Indian novelist of The God of Small Things; Booker Prize winner.
Salman Rushdie's controversial novel that provoked a fatwa from Ayatollah Khomeini.
Filipino nationalist novelist of Noli Me Tangere, whose execution helped spark the Philippine Revolution.
Classical Dravidian language literary tradition of South India and Sri Lanka.
Aravind Adiga's Booker-winning novel narrated by a servant-turned-entrepreneur in India.
Indian novelist known for the fictional South Indian town of Malgudi.
Vikram Seth's sprawling novel of a mother seeking a husband for her daughter in post-independence India.
Pakistani novelist of The Reluctant Fundamentalist and Exit West.
Telepathic protagonist of Rushdie's Midnight's Children, born at the exact moment of India's independence.
Southeast Asian nation home to novelist Pramoedya Ananta Toer.
R.K. Narayan's fictional South Indian town, setting for many of his novels.
Vikram Seth's verse novel in Onegin stanzas about San Francisco yuppies.
Refers to Indian novelists Anita Desai and her daughter Kiran Desai of The Inheritance of Loss.
Sanskrit epic attributed to Valmiki, of Rama's quest to rescue Sita from Ravana.
Full name of the nation home to Tagore, Rushdie, and Roy.
Indian-Canadian Parsi novelist of A Fine Balance, set during the Emergency.
Island nation home to Michael Ondaatje and Shehan Karunatilaka.
Kiran Desai's Booker-winning novel of a judge and his granddaughter in the Himalayan foothills.
Rohinton Mistry's novel of four lives intertwined in an Indian city during the 1975 Emergency.
Indian novelist of the Ibis trilogy beginning with Sea of Poppies, set around the Opium Wars.
Indian-American novelist of Jasmine, about an immigrant woman's serial reinventions.
Indian novelist of the Booker-winning The Inheritance of Loss; daughter of Anita Desai.
Kamala Markandaya's novel of the peasant woman Rukmani enduring rural Indian hardship.
Indonesian novelist of the Buru Quartet, beginning with This Earth of Mankind, written in prison.
Rabindranath Tagore's play of Nandini defying a gold-mining king's exploitative city.
English writer of Kim and The Jungle Book, set in British India; Nobel laureate.
Tagore's novel of Bimala torn between her husband Nikhil and the radical nationalist Sandip.
Tagore's play of the dying boy Amal awaiting a letter from the king.
South Asian language of Ghalib's ghazals and Manto's Partition stories.
Indian novelist of Clear Light of Day and In Custody; mother of Kiran Desai.
Indian novelist whose The White Tiger, narrated by driver Balram, won the Booker.
Jhumpa Lahiri's Pulitzer-winning story collection on Indian and Indian-American lives.
Vast Sanskrit epic of the Kurukshetra war between Pandavas and Kauravas; contains the Bhagavad Gita.
Jose Rizal's anti-colonial novel of Crisostomo Ibarra, which helped ignite Philippine nationalism.
Full name of the nation home to Pramoedya Ananta Toer.
Refers to Arundhati Roy, Indian author of The God of Small Things.
Kalidasa's Sanskrit drama of a king who forgets his bride until a lost ring restores memory.
Legendary Sanskrit "first poet," traditional author of the Ramayana.
Southeast Asian nation of the verse classic The Tale of Kieu and Bao Ninh's war fiction.
Jhumpa Lahiri story of Miranda's affair with a married Bengali man.
Token by which King Dushyanta finally recognizes Shakuntala in Kalidasa's drama.
Kashmiri-American poet who championed the ghazal in English; wrote The Country Without a Post Office.
Vikram Seth's novel about a violinist reuniting with a lost love in the classical music world.
Idealistic reformist protagonist of Jose Rizal's Noli Me Tangere.
King who forgets, then remembers, his beloved in Kalidasa's Shakuntala.
Indian prime minister whose Emergency-era rule backdrops Rohinton Mistry's fiction.
Full name of the South Asian nation home to novelists like Mohsin Hamid.
Refers to Bharati Mukherjee's novel Jasmine, about an Indian immigrant's reinvention in America.
Bengali "rebel poet" known for revolutionary and anti-colonial verse.
Ghost photographer protagonist of Shehan Karunatilaka's The Seven Moons of Maali Almeida.
16th-century Hindu mystic poet devoted to Krishna, celebrated in bhakti poetry.
Preeminent Urdu and Persian ghazal poet of Mughal-era India.
Struggling Trinidadian Indian protagonist of Naipaul's A House for Mr Biswas.
Refers to Bharati Mukherjee, Indian-American novelist known for Jasmine.
South Asian nation home to novelists like Mohsin Hamid.
Hero of the Ramayana, avatar of Vishnu who rescues his wife Sita from Ravana.
Urdu master of Partition stories, including the asylum tale "Toba Tek Singh."
Heroine of Kalidasa's drama, forgotten by King Dushyanta until a ring restores his memory.
Full name of the author of Midnight's Children and The Satanic Verses.
Sanskrit drama attributed to Shudraka, of the poor merchant Charudatta and courtesan Vasantasena.
Rushdie's novel of Moraes Zogoiby and a Bombay spice-trading family's decline.
Island nation of Jose Rizal, whose novels helped spark its revolution against Spain.

sections.name: East Asian Literature
sections.blurb: Chinese, Japanese, and Korean traditions range from classical Tang poetry and the Four Great Classical Novels to Kawabata and Murakami.

entries.note: Japanese novelist of The Temple of the Golden Pavilion and the Sea of Fertility tetralogy; died by ritual suicide.
Japanese short-story writer of "Rashomon" and "In a Grove," source for Kurosawa's film; died by suicide young.
Japanese Nobel laureate of Snow Country, Thousand Cranes, and The Sound of the Mountain; New Sensationalism.
Japanese postmodernist of Norwegian Wood, Kafka on the Shore, and 1Q84; magical realist elements.
Tang dynasty Chinese poet known for romantic, often drunken verse; frequently paired with rival Du Fu.
Edo-period Japanese haiku master; wrote the travel account The Narrow Road to the Deep North.
Home to Murakami, Kawabata, Mishima, and classical Heian court literature.
Founder of modern Chinese vernacular fiction; wrote "The True Story of Ah Q" during the May Fourth Movement.
Home to classical Tang poetry, the Four Great Classical Novels, and modern writers like Lu Xun and Mo Yan.
One of China's Four Great Classical Novels; bandit-heroes of Liangshan Marsh rebel against corrupt officials.
Qing-dynasty Chinese novel and one of the Four Great Classical Novels; centers on Jia Baoyu and the Jia family's decline.
Japanese surrealist/absurdist novelist known for existential fables about isolation.
Edo-period Japanese playwright of bunraku puppet and kabuki plays; wrote the Love Suicides dramas.
Tang dynasty Chinese poet known for socially engaged, historically grounded verse; often paired with Li Bai.
Ming-dynasty Chinese novel of the monkey king Sun Wukong escorting a monk to retrieve Buddhist scriptures.
Meiji-era Japanese novelist of Kokoro and I Am a Cat; central figure of modern Japanese literature.
Japanese novelist of The Makioka Sisters and Some Prefer Nettles; wrote the essay In Praise of Shadows.
Japanese Nobel laureate of A Personal Matter and Nip the Buds, Shoot the Kids; wrote about his disabled son.
Chinese Nobel laureate known for Red Sorghum; blends folk tales with historical narrative.
Ming-dynasty Chinese historical novel of warlords Cao Cao, Liu Bei, and the fall of the Han.
Home to modern Korean writers including Han Kang.
Sei Shonagon's Heian-era Japanese collection of lists and observations from court life; a zuihitsu.
Language of the Four Great Classical Novels and Tang dynasty poetry.
Japanese short poetic form perfected by Matsuo Basho and Kobayashi Issa.
Japanese puppet theater associated with playwright Chikamatsu Monzaemon.
Classical masked Japanese musical drama form, associated with playwright Zeami Motokiyo.
Full name of the nation home to Lu Xun, Mo Yan, and the Four Great Classical Novels.
Han Kang's Booker International-winning Korean novel of a woman who stops eating meat.
Chinese-French Nobel laureate of Soul Mountain and the absurdist play The Bus Stop.
Recurring motif tied to Japanese bunraku theater and Chikamatsu's plays.
Murasaki Shikibu's Heian-era Japanese novel about the courtier Hikaru Genji; often called the first novel.
Haruki Murakami's novel of parallel Tokyo realities with two moons, following Aomame and Tengo.
Language of Murakami, Kawabata, and classical court literature like The Tale of Genji.
Haruki Murakami's novel intertwining a runaway teen and an old man who talks to cats.
The trickster Monkey King of Journey to the West, escorting a monk to retrieve Buddhist scriptures.
Kenzaburo Oe's autobiographical novel about a father grappling with his brain-damaged newborn son.
Language of modern Korean writers including Han Kang.
Chinese Communist leader whose Cultural Revolution shaped much 20th-century Chinese literature.
Haruki Murakami's coming-of-age novel of Toru Watanabe's romantic entanglements in 1960s Tokyo.

entries.topic: The Narrow Road to the Deep North
entries.answerline: The Narrow Road to the Deep North
entries.note: Matsuo Basho's haibun travel account of a journey through northern Japan.
Japanese literary genre of loosely connected essays and observations, exemplified by Sei Shonagon's Pillow Book.
Ryunosuke Akutagawa's short story of conflicting accounts at a ruined gate, basis for Kurosawa's film.
South Korean novelist of The Vegetarian; won the International Booker Prize and later the Nobel.
Japanese novelist of The Makioka Sisters and Some Prefer Nettles.
Gao Xingjian's autobiographical novel of a journey through rural China after a false cancer diagnosis.
Golden age of Chinese poetry, era of Li Bai, Du Fu, and Wang Wei.
Kenzaburo Oe's novel of two brothers returning to their Shikoku village and its violent history.
Liu Cixin's Chinese science-fiction novel of first contact with the alien Trisolarans; Hugo winner.
Lu Xun's deluded everyman who reframes every humiliation as a "spiritual victory."
Cunning Han warlord antagonist of Romance of the Three Kingdoms.
Mao-era upheaval backdropping much modern Chinese fiction, including works by Yu Hua and Dai Sijie.
Alternate romanization of Li Bai, Tang dynasty poet of wine and moonlight.
Jun'ichiro Tanizaki's novel of a failing marriage torn between Western modernity and Japanese tradition.
Tanizaki's novel of four Osaka sisters seeking a husband for shy Yukiko in prewar Japan.
Tang dynasty poet-painter known for serene Buddhist nature poetry.
Chinese novelist of To Live and Chronicle of a Blood Merchant, chronicling Mao-era suffering.
Akutagawa story of a samurai's death told in contradictory testimonies; basis of Kurosawa's Rashomon.
Literary language of Tang poetry and the Confucian classics.
Chinese-American novelist of Waiting, about a doctor's eighteen-year wait to divorce and remarry.
The "Shining Prince," amorous courtier hero of Murasaki Shikibu's Tale of Genji.
Stylized Japanese popular theater form, adapting Chikamatsu's plays alongside bunraku.
Japanese-born British Nobel laureate of The Remains of the Day and Never Let Me Go.
Heian court lady author of The Tale of Genji; also names a character in the novel.
Kawabata's novel of Tokyo dilettante Shimamura's affair with the geisha Komako in a hot-spring town.

entries.topic: The Plum in the Golden Vase
entries.answerline: The Plum in the Golden Vase
entries.note: Ming-dynasty erotic novel Jin Ping Mei, chronicling the merchant Ximen Qing's household.
Kawabata's novel of the aging Shingo, haunted by death and drawn to his daughter-in-law.

entries.topic: The Temple of the Golden Pavilion
entries.answerline: The Temple of the Golden Pavilion
entries.note: Mishima's novel of a stuttering acolyte who burns down Kyoto's Kinkaku-ji temple.
Late autobiographical Akutagawa story of hallucinated spinning gears presaging his suicide.
Ezra Pound's Cathay adaptation of a Li Bai poem in a young wife's voice.
Japanese mystery pioneer whose pen name puns on Edgar Allan Poe; created detective Akechi.
Shanghai-born writer of Love in a Fallen City and Lust, Caution.
Han-dynasty Chinese "rhapsody" form blending prose and verse in ornate description.
Japanese form mixing prose and haiku, used by Basho in The Narrow Road to the Deep North.
Pampered heir born with a jade in his mouth, protagonist of Dream of the Red Chamber.
Haiku master known for tender, humorous poems about small creatures like snails and flies.
Meiji-era Japanese writer-surgeon of The Wild Geese and "The Dancing Girl."

entries.topic: Nip the Buds, Shoot the Kids
entries.answerline: Nip the Buds, Shoot the Kids
entries.note: Kenzaburo Oe's debut novel of reformatory boys abandoned in a plague-stricken village.
Osamu Dazai's novel of Yozo's masked alienation, declaring himself "disqualified as a human being."
Mo Yan's Shandong family saga of bandits, distillers, and resistance against Japanese invasion.
Japanese collaborative linked-verse form whose opening stanza evolved into the haiku.
Japanese warrior class recurring across Akutagawa's stories and Edo-period drama.
Shusaku Endo's novel of a Jesuit priest facing apostasy during Japan's persecution of Christians.
Chinese dynasty of ci lyric poetry, including the poet Li Qingzhao.
First novel of Mishima's Sea of Fertility, on Kiyoaki's doomed love for Satoko.
Classical 31-syllable Japanese court verse form, ancestor of the haiku.
Gao Xingjian's absurdist play of passengers waiting years for a bus that never stops.
Mishima's final tetralogy tracing apparent reincarnations observed by Shigekuni Honda.

entries.topic: The True Story of Ah Q
entries.answerline: The True Story of Ah Q
entries.note: Lu Xun's novella satirizing self-deceiving "spiritual victories" in pre-revolutionary China.
Murakami's novel of Toru Okada searching for his wife, descending into a dry well.
Kawabata's novel entangling tea-ceremony aesthetics with Kikuji's affairs.
Classical Japanese court poetry, the 31-syllable form central to Heian literature.
Akutagawa story of the painter Yoshihide, whose daughter burns in the carriage he must depict.
Creature that leaps into an old pond in Matsuo Basho's most famous haiku.
Chinese capital, setting and backdrop for much modern Chinese literature.
Traditional Chinese musical theater form incorporating stylized drama and song.
Yukio Mishima's autobiographical novel about a young man's closeted homosexuality in wartime Japan.
Disabled composer son of Kenzaburo Oe, inspiration for A Personal Matter.
Natsume Soseki's satirical novel narrated by a nameless cat observing Meiji-era Japanese society.
Edo-period Japanese writer known for tales of the pleasure quarters and merchant life.
Jun'ichiro Tanizaki's essay celebrating traditional Japanese aesthetics of shadow and subtlety.
Name-order variant of Yasunari Kawabata, Japanese Nobel laureate of Snow Country and Thousand Cranes.
Korean poet known for vast output, including the multi-volume Maninbo cycle.
Natsume Soseki's novel about a young man's friendship with an enigmatic, guilt-haunted "Sensei."
Character possessed by a jealous spirit in a Noh play adapted from The Tale of Genji.
Full honorific name of the Heian-era author of The Tale of Genji.
Recurring motif of Chikamatsu's bunraku/kabuki plays, as in The Love Suicides at Sonezaki.
Attributed author of the Chinese classical novel Romance of the Three Kingdoms.
Refers to Haruki Murakami, Japanese postmodernist novelist of Norwegian Wood and 1Q84.
Heian-era Japanese court lady credited with writing The Tale of Genji.
Classical masked Japanese musical drama form, associated with playwright Zeami Motokiyo.
Japanese novelist of No Longer Human, chronicling a man's alienation and self-destruction.
Min Jin Lee's novel following a Korean family's generations in Japan amid discrimination.
Heian court lady whose Pillow Book collects lists and observations; rival of Murasaki Shikibu.
Rationalist observer who tracks his friend's reincarnations across Mishima's Sea of Fertility.
Japanese Catholic novelist of Silence, on Jesuit persecution in 17th-century Japan.
The Shijing, oldest Chinese poetry anthology, traditionally compiled by Confucius.
Chikamatsu bunraku tragedy of paper merchant Jihei and the courtesan Koharu.
Chikamatsu puppet play of Tokubei and Ohatsu's double suicide in a forest shrine.
Wu Jingzi's Qing-dynasty satire of the imperial examination system and its strivers.
Japanese novelist of The Housekeeper and the Professor and The Memory Police.
Yuan-dynasty Chinese four-act variety drama form, as in The Orphan of Zhao's era.
Master playwright-theorist who codified Noh drama and its aesthetic of yugen.

sections.name: Caribbean Literature
sections.blurb: Anglophone and Francophone island writers -- Walcott, Naipaul, Cesaire -- explore colonialism, creolization, and diaspora.

entries.note: Saint Lucian Nobel poet of the epic Omeros; also wrote Dream on Monkey Mountain and "A Far Cry from Africa."
Trinidadian-British Nobel laureate of A House for Mr Biswas, A Bend in the River, and Miguel Street.
Martinican poet and co-founder of Negritude; wrote the anti-colonial Notebook of a Return to My Native Land.
Derek Walcott's Homeric epic poem retelling the Iliad and Odyssey through Saint Lucian fishermen.
Caribbean island home to writers exploring creolization and colonial legacy.
V.S. Naipaul's novel of a shopkeeper in a newly independent, decaying Central African nation.
Dominican-American novelist of The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao, narrated by Yunior.
Antiguan-American writer of Annie John and Lucy; explores mother-daughter conflict and colonialism.
Aime Cesaire's postcolonial rewriting of Shakespeare's play, recasting Caliban as an anti-colonial rebel.
Full name of the Saint Lucian Nobel poet who wrote Omeros.
Barbadian poet known for developing "nation language" poetics rooted in Caribbean speech.
Jean Rhys's prequel to Jane Eyre, giving the Creole "madwoman" Antoinette Cosway her own story.
Island home of poet Kamau Brathwaite and novelist George Lamming.
Derek Walcott's play about the charcoal burner Makak's visionary dream of returning to Africa.
Haitian-American author of Breath, Eyes, Memory and Krik? Krak!
Puerto Rican writer known for Spanglish and experimental works like Yo-Yo Boing! and United States of Banana.
Full name of V.S. Naipaul, Trinidadian-British Nobel laureate of A House for Mr Biswas.
Derek Walcott poem weighing Odysseus's homecoming against ordinary Caribbean life.
Refers to Derek Walcott, Saint Lucian Nobel poet of Omeros.
Caribbean nation under Trujillo, setting of Oscar Wao and The Feast of the Goat.
Barbadian novelist of In the Castle of My Skin, on colonial childhood.
Nation of the revolution depicted in Carpentier's Kingdom of This World; home of Danticat.
V.S. Naipaul's Booker-winning work of linked narratives about displacement, centered in an African state.
Jamaican novelist whose A Brief History of Seven Killings won the Booker Prize.
Naipaul's Trinidadian protagonist whose lifelong quest is a house of his own.
Derek Walcott poem on the Mau Mau uprising and being "divided to the vein" between loyalties.
Spelling variant of Kamau Brathwaite, Barbadian poet of nation language.
Jamaica Kincaid's novel of a young Antiguan au pair's cold-eyed view of her American employers.
V.S. Naipaul's linked stories of eccentric residents on a Port of Spain street.
Caribbean island home to writers like Giannina Braschi.
Full name of the nation whose revolution Carpentier depicted in The Kingdom of This World.

entries.topic: The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao
entries.answerline: The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao
entries.note: Junot Diaz's Pulitzer novel of a Dominican nerd and his family's fuku curse under Trujillo.
Edwidge Danticat's novel of a Haitian girl reunited with her mother in New York.
Trinidadian writer and historian of The Black Jacobins, on the Haitian Revolution.
Enslaved figure of Shakespeare's Tempest, recast as an anti-colonial rebel in Cesaire's version.
Refers to Aime Cesaire, Martinican poet and co-founder of Negritude.
Guadeloupean novelist of Segu and I, Tituba, Black Witch of Salem.
Region of Walcott, Naipaul, and creolized literary traditions.
Home island of V.S. Naipaul and C.L.R. James.
Twin-island nation home to V.S. Naipaul and C.L.R. James.
Junot Diaz's recurring Dominican-American narrator, teller of Oscar Wao's story.

sections.name: Commonwealth Literature: Australia, Canada & New Zealand
sections.blurb: A smaller but distinct Anglophone Commonwealth tradition, from Mansfield and White to Atwood, Munro, and Peter Carey.

entries.note: Home to Patrick White, Peter Carey, and bush-ballad poets like Banjo Paterson.
Home to Katherine Mansfield and the Booker-winning The Bone People.
Home to Margaret Atwood, Alice Munro, and Michael Ondaatje.
New Zealand modernist short-story writer; wrote "Bliss" and "The Garden Party."
Canadian novelist of The Handmaid's Tale and Oryx and Crake; dystopian and feminist themes.
Australian novelist of Oscar and Lucinda; two-time Booker Prize winner.
Canadian Nobel laureate short-story writer; wrote "Dance of the Happy Shades"; Southern Ontario Gothic.
Full name of the nation home to Patrick White and Peter Carey.
Michael Ondaatje's Booker-winning novel of a burned patient and companions in an Italian villa during WWII.
Katherine Mansfield short story contrasting a wealthy family's party with a neighbor's death.
Refers to Patrick White, Australian Nobel laureate novelist of Voss.
Sri Lankan-Canadian novelist of The English Patient; also wrote the memoir Running in the Family.
Australian Nobel laureate novelist of Voss, about an explorer's doomed expedition.
Indigenous New Zealand people and language central to Keri Hulme's The Bone People.
Australian author of My Brilliant Career; namesake of Australia's top literary award.
Australian bush poet of "Waltzing Matilda" and "The Man from Snowy River."
Peter Carey's Booker-winning novel of two gamblers transporting a glass church across Australia.
Australian novelist of Schindler's Ark, the Booker winner behind Schindler's List.
Katherine Mansfield story of Bertha Young's euphoria shattered by discovering her husband's affair.
New Zealand novelist whose gold-rush novel The Luminaries won the Booker Prize.
Full name of the Australian Nobel laureate who wrote Voss.
Full name of the Canadian Nobel short-story writer of Dance of the Happy Shades.
Canadian poet-classicist of Autobiography of Red, reworking the Geryon myth.
Yann Martel's Booker-winning novel of a boy adrift with the tiger Richard Parker.
Full name of the Canadian author of The Handmaid's Tale.
German industrialist who saved Jewish workers, subject of Keneally's Schindler's Ark.
Theocratic regime of Atwood's Handmaid's Tale, where handmaids bear children for commanders.
Outback setting mythologized in bush ballads by Paterson and Lawson.
Title story of Alice Munro's debut collection, at a piano recital for disabled children.
Australian outlaw figures like Ned Kelly, recurring in Australian literary and folk tradition.
Fictitious poet invented as a modernist-poetry hoax by two Australian writers in 1943.
Miles Franklin's novel of a headstrong young woman resisting marriage in rural Australia.
Australian bushranger/outlaw figure, subject of Peter Carey's True History of the Kelly Gang.
Margaret Atwood's dystopian novel of bioengineering catastrophe, narrated by "Snowman."
Full name of the two-time Booker-winning Australian novelist of Oscar and Lucinda.
Keri Hulme's Booker-winning novel of the reclusive Kerewin, a mute boy, and Maori identity.
Patrick White's novel of a German explorer's doomed trek across the Australian interior.

sections.name: Classical Antiquity
sections.blurb: Greek tragedy and comedy, Roman poetry, and ancient Near Eastern epic supply a comparative layer beneath the modern unit.

entries.note: Sophocles's tragedy of a woman who defies King Creon to bury her brother Polynices.
Ancient Greek lyric poet from Lesbos known for passionate, fragmentary love poetry.
Ancient Greek tragedian of Medea and The Bacchae, known for psychologically complex characters.
Ancient Mesopotamian language of Enheduanna's hymns and the earliest Gilgamesh poems.
Language and tradition of hieroglyphic texts like the Book of the Dead and love poetry.
Ancient Greek epic poet of the Iliad and Odyssey; model for Walcott's Omeros.
Euripides's tragedy in which Dionysus drives Theban women to tear King Pentheus apart.
Homer's epic of Achilles's wrath during the Trojan War.
Roman neoteric poet of passionate short lyrics to his beloved Lesbia.
Akkadian high priestess of Ur, hymnist of Inanna and history's first named author.
Euripides's tragedy of the sorceress who kills her own children to avenge Jason's betrayal.
Roman poet of the Aeneid, epic of Aeneas's journey from Troy to found Rome.
Language of Homer, Sappho, and Athenian tragedy; a model for later world epics like Omeros.
Aristophanes's Old Comedy play satirizing Socrates and sophistic education.
Ancient Mesopotamian epic of a Sumerian king's quest for immortality after his friend Enkidu's death.
Aristophanes's Old Comedy play about Dionysus's journey to the underworld to retrieve a tragic poet.
Full name of the Roman neoteric poet known for passionate lyric poems to Lesbia.
Roman lyric poet known for the Odes and the Ars Poetica.
Aristophanes's comedy in which Athenian and Spartan women withhold sex to end the Peloponnesian War.
Sophocles's tragic king who unwittingly kills his father and marries his mother.
Roman poet of the Metamorphoses, a compendium of mythological transformation stories.
Athenian tragedian of Oedipus Rex and Antigone.
Aristophanes's earliest surviving comedy, in which a farmer makes a private peace with Sparta.
Homer's epic of Odysseus's ten-year homecoming to Ithaca and Penelope.
Euripides's tragedy of Hecuba and the captive women after Troy's fall.
Ancient Near Eastern genre of proverbs and instruction, as in Egyptian and Sumerian texts.

sections.name: Continental European Literature
sections.blurb: Kafka, Tolstoy, Goethe, Camus, and other non-English-language European writers recur as a comparative layer within the unit.

entries.note: Language of Flaubert, Voltaire, Sartre, and Camus, as well as Francophone Caribbean and African writers.
German playwright who developed Epic Theatre and the alienation effect; wrote Mother Courage.
Henrik Ibsen's play Ghosts, about hereditary syphilis and hypocrisy in a Norwegian family.
French realist novelist of Madame Bovary, about a doctor's wife's affairs and disillusionment.
Jose Saramago's novel of a contagious epidemic of white blindness and societal collapse.
Prague German-language writer of The Metamorphosis and The Trial; namesake of Murakami's Kafka on the Shore.
Home of Cervantes and Lorca; colonial power shaping Latin American literature.
Cervantes's novel of a deluded knight-errant and squire Sancho Panza; touchstone for Borges's Pierre Menard.
Storm giant of the Cape of Storms in Camoes's Portuguese epic The Lusiads.
Home of Dante, Manzoni's The Betrothed, and Italo Calvino.
French existentialist of Nausea and No Exit; declined the Nobel Prize.
Russian author of "The Overcoat" and Dead Souls; namesake of the protagonist in Lahiri's The Namesake.
Camus's novel of Meursault, who kills an Arab on an Algiers beach and faces absurd judgment.
Gogol story of a clerk's descent into madness; Lu Xun reused the title for China's first modern story.
Full name of the Russian poet considered founder of modern Russian literature; wrote Eugene Onegin.
French Nobel laureate novelist and satirist known for Penguin Island.
Leo Tolstoy's novel of a married Russian aristocrat's ruinous affair with Count Vronsky.
Spanish poet and playwright of Blood Wedding, executed during the Spanish Civil War.
Medieval French poet known for the vagabond, criminal life reflected in his ballades.
English novelist of The Power and the Glory and The Quiet American, exploring faith and colonialism.
Polish-American Yiddish-language Nobel laureate known for tales of Jewish life in Eastern Europe.
German polymath of Faust and The Sorrows of Young Werther; central to Weimar Classicism.
Portuguese Nobel laureate novelist of Blindness, about a contagious epidemic of white blindness.
Russian novelist of Anna Karenina and War and Peace; late convert to Christian anarchism.
German composer occasionally invoked as a comparison figure in common-link tossups.
Gustave Flaubert's novel of a provincial doctor's wife's affairs and disillusionment.
Andre Malraux's novel of revolutionaries during the 1927 Shanghai uprising.
Full name of the Spanish author of Don Quixote.
German political movement whose Holocaust backdrops works like Schindler's Ark.
Karen Blixen's (Isak Dinesen's) memoir of running a coffee farm in colonial Kenya.
Language of Nobel poets Czeslaw Milosz and Wislawa Szymborska.
French critic of Mythologies who proclaimed "The Death of the Author."
Irish absurdist of Waiting for Godot, an influence on Gao Xingjian's Bus Stop.
Alessandro Manzoni's Italian novel of lovers Renzo and Lucia separated in plague-era Lombardy.
European nation whose colonial rule of Indonesia backdrops Multatuli's Max Havelaar.
German Nobel novelist of The Magic Mountain, Death in Venice, and Buddenbrooks.
French Enlightenment satirist of Candide, mocking optimism through Pangloss.

sections.name: Movements, Motifs & Common Links
sections.blurb: Recurring objects, themes, and literary movements -- magical realism, Negritude, the Boom, common-link animals and colors -- tie works together across regions.

entries.note: Genre blending realistic narrative with fantastical elements; defines the Latin American Boom and beyond.
